importanceReasons: citations=189; influential=13; recognized venue; BCI term matches=2; title BCI matches=1; open PDF metadata
title: Visual spatial attention control in an independent brain-computer interface
authors: S. Kelly, E. Lalor, C. Finucane, G. McDarby, R. Reilly
venue: IEEE Transactions on Biomedical Engineering
abstract: This paper presents a novel brain computer interface (BCI) design employing visual evoked potential (VEP) modulations in a paradigm involving no dependency on peripheral muscles or nerves. The system utilizes electrophysiological correlates of visual spatial attention mechanisms, the self-regulation of which is naturally developed through continuous application in everyday life. An interface involving real-time biofeedback is described, demonstrating reduced training time in comparison to existing BCIs based on self-regulation paradigms. Subjects were cued to covertly attend to a sequence of letters superimposed on a flicker stimulus in one visual field while ignoring a similar stimulus of a different flicker frequency in the opposite visual field. Classification of left/right spatial attention is achieved by extracting steady-state visual evoked potentials (SSVEPs) elicited by the stimuli. Six out of eleven physically and neurologically healthy subjects demonstrate reliable control in binary decision-making, achieving at least 75% correct selections in at least one of only five sessions, each of approximately 12-min duration. The highest-performing subject achieved over 90% correct selections in each of four sessions. This independent BCI may provide a new method of real-time interaction for those with little or no peripheral control, with the added advantage of requiring only brief training.

importanceReasons: citations=303; influential=6; recognized venue; BCI term matches=1; title BCI matches=1; open PDF metadata
title: Cortical Ensemble Adaptation to Represent Velocity of an Artificial Actuator Controlled by a Brain-Machine Interface
authors: M. Lebedev, J. Carmena, Joseph E. O’Doherty, M. Zacksenhouse, C. Henriquez, J. Príncipe, et al.
venue: Journal of Neuroscience
abstract: Monkeys can learn to directly control the movements of an artificial actuator by using a brain-machine interface (BMI) driven by the activity of a sample of cortical neurons. Eventually, they can do so without moving their limbs. Neuronal adaptations underlying the transition from control of the limb to control of the actuator are poorly understood. Here, we show that rapid modifications in neuronal representation of velocity of the hand and actuator occur in multiple cortical areas during the operation of a BMI. Initially, monkeys controlled the actuator by moving a hand-held pole. During this period, the BMI was trained to predict the actuator velocity. As the monkeys started using their cortical activity to control the actuator, the activity of individual neurons and neuronal populations became less representative of the animal's hand movements while representing the movements of the actuator. As a result of this adaptation, the animals could eventually stop moving their hands yet continue to control the actuator. These results show that, during BMI control, cortical ensembles represent behaviorally significant motor parameters, even if these are not associated with movements of the animal's own limb.

importanceReasons: citations=80; influential=5; BCI term matches=2; title BCI matches=1; open PDF metadata
title: Feature Selection and Blind Source Separation in an EEG-Based Brain-Computer Interface
authors: D. A. Peterson, James N. Knight, M. Kirby, C. Anderson, M. Thaut
venue: EURASIP Journal on Advances in Signal Processing
abstract: Most EEG-based BCI systems make use of well-studied patterns of brain activity. However, those systems involve tasks that indirectly map to simple binary commands such as "yes" or "no" or require many weeks of biofeedback training. We hypothesized that signal processing and machine learning methods can be used to discriminate EEG in a direct "yes"/"no" BCI from a single session. Blind source separation (BSS) and spectral transformations of the EEG produced a 180-dimensional feature space. We used a modified genetic algorithm (GA) wrapped around a support vector machine (SVM) classifier to search the space of feature subsets. The GA-based search found feature subsets that outperform full feature sets and random feature subsets. Also, BSS transformations of the EEG outperformed the original time series, particularly in conjunction with a subset search of both spaces. The results suggest that BSS and feature selection can be used to improve the performance of even a "direct," single-session BCI.

title: Wavelet-based Processing of EEG Data for Brain-Computer Interfaces
authors: D. Cheng, V. D'Amato, Vittorio Murino
venue: Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition
abstract: Brain-Computer Interfaces based on non-invasive electroencephalographic (EEG) signals were recently made practical through sophisticated algorithms and clever systems, in such a way that the dream of effortlessly translating volition into action is coming true, albeit in a limited way. However, a low signal-to-noise ratio and the presence of frequent artefacts, such as eye blinks, contaminate the recordings and make the recognition of the underlying mental processes difficult. In this study, a novel waveletbased signal processing technique, ContinuousWavelet Regression, has been applied to refine EEG data in a wellknown setting. The recordings of spontaneous (i.e., asynchronous) signals of subjects performing highly different cognitive tasks have been processed by our algorithm, and then analyzed and classified, obtaining very promising results as compared with those obtained by previous studies.

importanceReasons: citations=0; influential=0; dataset/benchmark; clinical/rehab relevance; BCI term matches=3; title BCI matches=2; open PDF metadata
title: Enhanced Feature Selection for Motor Imagery-Based Brain-Computer Interface: A Comparative Study of Hippopotamus Optimization Algorithm with Riemannian and CSP Techniques
authors: Satvika S S, Puspita Biswas, Vishnu Priya R, J. J, V. M.
venue: 2026 3rd International Conference on Integrated Intelligence and Communication Systems (ICIICS)
abstract: EEG-based motor imagery (MI) classification is a critical component of Brain-Computer Interface (BCI) systems for assistive and rehabilitation applications. However, the nonstationary and noisy nature of EEG signals poses significant challenges for achieving robust classification performance. This paper investigates three hybrid MI-BCI pipelines that integrate spatial feature extraction, machine learning classifiers, and metaheuristic-based feature selection. Common Spatial Pattern (CSP) and Riemannian Geometry are employed for feature extraction, while Support Vector Machine and Random Forest classifiers are optimized using the Hippopotamus Optimization (HO) algorithm. Experimental evaluation on a public EEG motor imagery dataset demonstrates that the CSP+SVM+HO pipeline achieves the best performance, with an accuracy of 95 % and an F1-score of 0.95. The Riemannian Geometry+SVM+HO pipeline achieves 92.5 % accuracy, whereas the Riemannian Geometry + Random Forest +HO pipeline yields 72.5 %. These results highlight the effectiveness of optimized spatial filtering and feature selection in enhancing MI-BCI classification and support the development of reliable BCI systems.

importanceReasons: citations=0; influential=0; review/survey; modern ML method; BCI term matches=3; title BCI matches=1; open PDF metadata
title: An Analytical Study of Deep Learning Techniques in Motor Imagery Eeg Signal Processing
authors: V. R, K. A
venue: 2026 3rd International Conference on Integrated Intelligence and Communication Systems (ICIICS)
abstract: The application of Brain-Computer Interface (BCI) depends largely on Motor Imagery (MI) based electroncephalography (EEG) to allow communication and control to people with motor loss. Deep Learning has now become a potent method in handling the MI-EEG signal which automatically bypasses complicated spatial, spectral and time features and crosses the limitations of the conventional machine learning methods. Based on the in-depth examination of the deep learning methods adopted in the processing of MI-EEG, the review outlines the same on a number of architectures such as the Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs), Transformers, and composite models. It also points out the key issues in signal processing of MI-EEG, evaluation of the performance and benchmarking evaluation. Our review has been an informative aid to the researchers and doctors in choosing the appropriate deep learning models, defining the areas that have been a challenge in deep learning and outlying the future directions to be researched.

importanceReasons: citations=0; influential=0; dataset/benchmark; modern ML method; BCI term matches=3; title BCI matches=1; open PDF metadata
title: Multi-Scale Spatio-Temporal Network with Frequency-Spatial Parallel Attention for Motor Imagery Classification
authors: R. Sait, Mahesh Basavaraj, V. Y, P. G, Nutan Sitanshu
venue: 2026 3rd International Conference on Integrated Intelligence and Communication Systems (ICIICS)
abstract: Motor Imagery (MI) classification is important component of Brain-Computer Interface (BCI) system to identify and interpret brain signal produced during visualization of MI tasks by a subject. This task is challenging due to the non-stationarity of EEG signals, low signal-to-noise ratio and complex overlapping of spatial-spectral patterns. However, traditional Deep Learning models often suffer from limited capability in modeling global frequency contexts, loss of fine-grained spatial details and multi-scale temporal features. To overcome this limitation, paper offers a Multi-Scale SpatioTemporal network with Frequency-Spatial Parallel Attention (MSST-FSPA) model for motor imagery (MI) task classification. The MSST-FSPA model integrates Frequency Channel Attention (FCA) and Spatial Detail Enhancement (SDE) modules. The FCA module effectively captures global contextual dependencies through spectral-wise attention, while the SDE enhances local spatial feature refinement using depthwise convolutions. Experimental results show the MSSTFSPA model effectively achieves 98.60% on the BCIC-IV-2a dataset and 98.80% on the BCIC-IV-2b dataset. These results confirm that the MSST-FSPA model effectively balance classification performance and computational efficiency, making it suitable for BCI system.

importanceReasons: citations=0; influential=0; modern ML method; BCI term matches=2; open PDF metadata
title: CerebralChat: A Hybrid 3D-CNN and BiLSTM Model for Imagined Speech Recognition from EEG Signals
authors: Smitha B A, Mahantesh K, Dharini E, Impana P Nittur, S. Shetty, Shri Vidya V K
venue: 2026 3rd International Conference on Integrated Intelligence and Communication Systems (ICIICS)
abstract: Brain computer interfaces (BCIs) are emerging as transformative technologies, enabling direct communication between the human brain and external systems without muscular or verbal effort. Among various BCI paradigms, imagined speech recognition, which decodes silently thought words from neural activity, offers a natural and intuitive interaction pathway, particularly for individuals with speech or motor impairments. However, the non-stationary nature of EEG signals, low signal to noise ratio, and inter subject variability make accurate recognition a challenging task. A hybrid deep learning framework is proposed for decoding imagined speech from EEG signals. The proposed model integrates 3D Convolutional Neural Networks (3D-CNN) for spatial feature learning with Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (Bi-LSTM) networks to capture temporal dependencies across EEG sequences. EEG recordings are transformed into spatio-temporal topographic brain maps, preserving both spatial distribution and temporal dynamics. The presented framework demonstrates improved accuracy relative to recent state-of-theart methods. The proposed approach achieving an average accuracy of 84.67%. These findings highlight the potential of hybrid architectures for robust and real time BCI applications, paving the way for advanced assistive communication technologies.
